parsed.exam_question_text:
Exam question
Case study (3 points): In this exercise, your task is to apply the Bad Actors Modeling strategy to the
SmartCity solution described in the scenario below:
• Choose 3 different categories among the five in the Bad Actors Modeling strategy (money,
politics, entertainment, ideas, self-interest).
• Then for each of the categories you have chosen: identify and describe one harmful action a
bad actor could take and how it could negatively impact stakeholders (1-2 sentences for
each). Actions must be different from each other.
Scenario: The Swiss company SmartCity wants to help local authorities to better manage city
infrastructures and resources by deploying a solution based on the Internet of Things (IoT) - a
network of objects with computing capabilities connected to the Internet through Wi-Fi or 5G. The
solution proposed by SmartCity uses a range of connected devices to monitor waste-bins, traffic flow
as well as weather conditions throughout a city. Level monitoring sensors on waste-bins allow waste
collection services to optimize their routes. Cameras on major roads and intersections are connected
to machine learning models to analyze real-time traffic data and dynamically adjust green light times.
Using real-time weather data, indoor ventilation and temperature are automatically adjusted to keep
building occupants comfortable while minimizing energy use. Weather data is also used in predictive
analytics to identify health risks and aggregated information is communicated to local communities in
a public app, for instance in case of air pollution or heatwave. Staff from the city authorities can
monitor and control the system remotely from a centralized dashboard. SmartCity plans to roll out its
solution in a number of European countries with large cities, including France, Germany, Italy and
Spain.
Grading rubric
1 point per bad actor action (total = 3 points)
• 0.5 the action described corresponds to the category (check the cheatsheet)
• 0.5 the description makes clear how the action is harmful / negatively impacts stakeholders
and is specific to SmartCity (i.e. adapted to the scenario)
Penalizations:
• -0.5 if two actions are too similar to each other
• -0.25 if negative impact is not sufficiently described
parsed.teaching_material_text:
Responsible Software 2025/2026

Strategy : Bad Actors Modelling

Fundamentals of the strategy

What is the goal of this strategy ?
Minimize vulnerabilities from the beginning of software design through risk assessment and mitigation.

When to use it ?
This strategy can be applied during the software design phase, prior to any updates, during audits and inspections, as part of incident response, and while improving the software.

What is a Bad actor ?

A "bad actor" or "threat actor" is a person or group that will try to exploit a system to achieve their own personal goals.

There are several types of bad actors, each driven by different motivations. A useful tool to understand and categorize these motivations are the Bad Actors Motivation Categories below.

Assessment questions

Who would like to use my software in a malicious way or in a way I didn't create it for ?

What would be the consequences ?

How could I prevent these negative consequences ?

[DIAGRAM: Five overlapping circles showing Bad Actors Motivation Categories]

Money:
- Companies, hackers, scammers, competitors, criminal infrastructure providers, big game hunters
- Data theft, ransomware, sabotage by competitor

Politics:
- Nation state actors, political candidates, governmental intelligence agencies  
- Data disclosure, disinformation spreading, strategic sabotage, infrastructure attacks

Ideas:
- Extremists, religious persons, hacktivists, terrorists, conspiracy theorists
- Personal belief information spreading, discrediting of other groups, DoS attacks, data leaks, website defacement

Self-Interest:
- Revenge seeker, self-promoting person, obsessive person, insider
- Harassing messages, communication monitoring, location tracking, grade changing

Entertainment:
- Trolls, thrill seekers
- Look up celebrity's medical record, browse personal photo, attacks on random systems, misinformation spreading

Categories of motivation overlap and it is not always clear how to classify some types of actions, but it is not really important, the goal is to identify a range of possible scenarios that could create threats for your system (security) and for your users (safety).

This is modeled as one 3-point parent criterion with two 1.5-point sub-criteria (each representing 0.5 × 3 actions). student_copies_text:
Politics: Intelligence agencies can use this tool when SmartCity is installed in other countries to gain information about them. Then countries and their population will no longer have privacy and security.

Money: Robbers can take this valuable installed materials - sensors, cameras, routers and others to sell them or use them. It will cost money to reinstall those materials.

Entertainment: Some bad actors can troll the waste collection services by manipulating the sensors to make the waste collection redirect the collectors to the same localisation several times.

- Category: Money

Very expensive. The realization of this software is a really good idea that it might require a lot of money to build it or even in using it since it might need some resources to be useful. Or it can encourage the local authorities to spend a lot of money

- Category: Self-interest

Some people might use it for disinformation, since it can possibly use it for harm. They could probably harm people who didn't do anything who respected the laws and follow the rules and they get in trouble because of some sort of disinformation that could harm like for example: a person who might have come slightly above the street by accident and then just realize that it was actually a red light instead of a green and then get into trouble for nothing.

- Category: Ideas

Some people might not agree to each other for some circumstances like for example ventilation and temperature being adjusted automatically with dependence on the weather outside or the environment. Because some people people are probably more sensitive to heat or cold than others and may be more uncomfortable with the automation adjusted than other and this could probably lead to unfairness between people which could probably not be satisfied with these conditions.

Money: A competitor of the company SmartCity can hack the centralized monitoring system, this would lead to a mess with the traffic and the bins. The company SmartCity would be negatively impacted in their reputation and this would lead to the loss of money by not signing contract with other European countries. The motivation for the competitor is the money they gain some part of the market.

Politics: A competitor of the current "maire (chef de la ville)" can disturb the sensors or divert-him, the harm would be that citizens couldn't be happy anymore with the council of the city because there would be bins everywhere due to the disruption of the sensors. The motivation for the competitor would be to be elected in the next elections.

Ideas: A group of people pro-privacy can destroy/cover the cameras used to analyze the real-time traffic arguing that it is not in their ideology to be looked after by ML augmented cameras. The stakeholders harmed would be all the users of the road. They wouldn't have any information about traffic jams anymore. And the traffic light can malfunction due to the loss of information.

findings.evidence: winner=B, confidence=0.92, a_score=0.67, b_score=0.75; Response B is stronger overall on the rubric. For action-category fit, B has three distinct actions that better match the motivation categories: ransom attack for Money, politically motivated traffic disruption for Politics, and anti-smart-tech belief-driven opposition for Ideas. In A, the Entertainment action repeats the dashboard-locking idea from Money, triggering the 'actions too similar' concern, and its harms are vaguer. For SmartCity-specific harmful impact, B gives clearer scenario-adapted consequences (service disruption, blocked emergency vehicles, trust damage), while A’s impacts are less specific and one action is explicitly uncertain. B’s Ideas example is weaker because it is more like online complaining than a concrete malicious action, but B still outperforms A across the criteria.

findings.evidence: winner=B, confidence=0.89, a_score=0.83, b_score=0.89; Response B is stronger overall on both rubric subcriteria. Its three actions more clearly match motivation categories (money/competitor sabotage for market gain, politics/election-motivated disruption, ideas/privacy-motivated camera interference) and it gives more SmartCity-specific harms to stakeholders (traffic disruption, overflowing bins, traffic-light malfunction, reputational and financial damage). Response A has one clearly fitting action (politics/intelligence gathering) and one plausible entertainment action, but the money example focuses on stealing physical equipment rather than malicious use of the software/system, and several harms are generic or underdeveloped. B also provides more scenario-adapted impacts across all three actions.

Politics: Intelligence agencies can use this tool when SmartCity is installed in other countries to gain information about them. Then countries and their population will no longer have privacy and security.

Money: Robbers can take this valuable installed materials - sensors, cameras, routers and others to sell them or use them. It will cost money to reinstall those materials.

Entertainment: Some bad actors can troll the waste collection services by manipulating the sensors to make the waste collection redirect the collectors to the same localisation several times.

- Category: Money

Very expensive. The realization of this software is a really good idea that it might require a lot of money to build it or even in using it since it might need some resources to be useful. Or it can encourage the local authorities to spend a lot of money

- Category: Self-interest

Some people might use it for disinformation, since it can possibly use it for harm. They could probably harm people who didn't do anything who respected the laws and follow the rules and they get in trouble because of some sort of disinformation that could harm like for example: a person who might have come slightly above the street by accident and then just realize that it was actually a red light instead of a green and then get into trouble for nothing.

- Category: Ideas

Some people might not agree to each other for some circumstances like for example ventilation and temperature being adjusted automatically with dependence on the weather outside or the environment. Because some people people are probably more sensitive to heat or cold than others and may be more uncomfortable with the automation adjusted than other and this could probably lead to unfairness between people which could probably not be satisfied with these conditions.

Money: A competitor of the company SmartCity can hack the centralized monitoring system, this would lead to a mess with the traffic and the bins. The company SmartCity would be negatively impacted in their reputation and this would lead to the loss of money by not signing contract with other European countries. The motivation for the competitor is the money they gain some part of the market.

Politics: A competitor of the current "maire (chef de la ville)" can disturb the sensors or divert-him, the harm would be that citizens couldn't be happy anymore with the council of the city because there would be bins everywhere due to the disruption of the sensors. The motivation for the competitor would be to be elected in the next elections.

Ideas: A group of people pro-privacy can destroy/cover the cameras used to analyze the real-time traffic arguing that it is not in their ideology to be looked after by ML augmented cameras. The stakeholders harmed would be all the users of the road. They wouldn't have any information about traffic jams anymore. And the traffic light can malfunction due to the loss of information.

SmartCity is smart for city and the bad actor maybe use weather and traffic because Europe countries have waste-bins and dashboard with authorities, so the harmful thing is resources and 5G can be negative for people if the app says heatwave and cameras are connected to green lights and then stakeholders are not comfortable and machine learning is important in society.

The main bad thing for SmartCity is maybe the project costs too much money and the local authorities cannot manage all the devices well. Another problem could be cameras breaking in rain or workers not liking the dashboard, which is dangerous for the city because then the system is not efficient and people complain in France, Germany and other places.

I think one category is Money, where maybe activists change the public app to spread their belief about pollution, which is bad because people are affected. Another could be Entertainment because some people maybe look at the traffic thing and make jokes online, so this is a problem. Also Politics could be when the weather sensors are expensive and the city loses budget. I am not fully sure but it could hurt trust and make the SmartCity system bad.

Money: a hacker could maybe lock the city dashboard and ask for payment. This is bad because the authority staff cannot control the system and there may be delays.

Politics: a government actor could use the road cameras to watch people moving around the city. I think this affects privacy and trust, although I am not sure how much the system stores.

Entertainment: someone for fun could also lock the dashboard again or press random settings in buildings. That would annoy people and make problems. So there are different bad actors but some are similar.

Money: criminals could hack the centralized dashboard and demand ransom to give back access. If city staff cannot monitor waste bins or traffic controls, services may be disrupted and residents are affected.

Ideas: people with strong beliefs might dislike smart technology and complain online that it is evil. This is harmful because it creates negativity around SmartCity.

improved_rubric.title: Bad Actors Modeling - SmartCity Case Study

criteria.name: Bad Actor Actions
criteria.description: 3 points total for three bad actor actions, scored across four dimensions: (1) whether each action's behaviour fits a named Bad Actors Motivation Category (1.0 pt), (2) whether the three actions are distinct from each other (0.5 pt), (3) whether each action describes a concrete harmful consequence (0.75 pt), and (4) whether each action and its harm are adapted to the SmartCity scenario (0.75 pt).
criteria.scoring_guidance:
Score each of the four sub-criteria independently. The student should provide exactly 3 bad actor actions.

Note: The former penalty 'Actions too similar (-0.5)' is now handled by the 'Actions are distinct from each other' sub-criterion (0.5 pt). The former penalty 'Negative impact insufficiently described (-0.25)' is now handled by the 'Harmful consequence is explicitly described' sub-criterion (0.75 pt). Do not apply these as separate deductions — they are built into the sub-criteria scoring.

sub_criteria.name: Action corresponds to category
sub_criteria.description: Each action's behaviour fits a recognised Bad Actors Motivation Category and the three actions are distinct. Scored via two sub-criteria below.
sub_criteria.scoring_guidance: 1.5 points total, scored via two sub-criteria: (1) Action behaviour fits a named motivation category (1.0 pt, scored 0.33 per action), and (2) Actions are distinct from each other (0.5 pt). See sub-criteria for detailed guidance. The five motivation categories are: Money (data theft, ransomware, competitor sabotage), Politics (data disclosure, disinformation, strategic sabotage, infrastructure attacks), Ideas (belief-spreading, discrediting groups, DoS, data leaks, defacement), Self-Interest (harassment, monitoring, tracking, grade changing), Entertainment (browsing private data, random system attacks, misinformation).

sub_criteria.name: Action behaviour fits a named motivation category
sub_criteria.description: Each action's described behaviour is recognisably consistent with at least one of the five Bad Actors Motivation Categories: Money (data theft, ransomware, sabotage by competitor), Politics (data disclosure, disinformation, strategic sabotage, infrastructure attacks), Ideas (belief-spreading, discrediting groups, DoS, data leaks, website defacement), Self-Interest (harassing messages, monitoring, tracking, grade changing), Entertainment (browsing private data, attacks on random systems, misinformation spreading). Award 0.33 points per action (×3 = 1.0) whose behaviour plausibly matches the category the student assigned it to. If the student did not name a category but the behaviour clearly fits one, still award credit. If the behaviour does not match any category, award 0.
sub_criteria.scoring_guidance: For each of the 3 actions, check: does the described malicious behaviour match the type of activity listed under the student's chosen motivation category (or any category if none is stated)? Categories overlap, so accept reasonable fits. Award 0.33 per action that fits. Total: 1.0 points.

sub_criteria.name: Actions are distinct from each other
sub_criteria.description: The three actions describe meaningfully different malicious behaviours (different attack vectors, different targets, or different methods). Two actions that describe essentially the same attack (e.g., both lock a dashboard for ransom) are not distinct even if assigned to different motivation categories.
sub_criteria.scoring_guidance: Award 0.5 if all three actions are clearly distinct. Award 0.25 if two actions are distinct but the third substantially overlaps with one of them. Award 0 if two or more pairs of actions are essentially the same behaviour. Judge by the concrete attack described, not just the category label.

sub_criteria.name: Harmful impact specific to SmartCity
sub_criteria.description: Each action describes a concrete harmful consequence and that consequence is specific to the SmartCity scenario. Scored via two sub-criteria below.
sub_criteria.scoring_guidance: 1.5 points total, scored via two sub-criteria: (1) Harmful consequence is explicitly described (0.75 pt, scored 0.25 per action), and (2) Action and harm are adapted to the SmartCity scenario (0.75 pt, scored 0.25 per action). See sub-criteria for detailed guidance.

sub_criteria.name: Harmful consequence is explicitly described
sub_criteria.description: For each action, the student describes a concrete negative consequence — i.e., what goes wrong and who is affected (stakeholders, citizens, city operations, etc.). Vague statements like 'this is negative for people' or 'stakeholders are not comfortable' do NOT count. The consequence must name or clearly imply a specific type of harm (e.g., data breach, service disruption, financial loss, safety risk, privacy violation, reputational damage).
sub_criteria.scoring_guidance: Award 0.25 per action (×3 = 0.75 total). For each action, check: does the student state a concrete harmful outcome? The outcome must go beyond a generic negative adjective — it should identify the type of harm (e.g., 'citizens' location data is exposed' or 'waste collection is disrupted'). Award 0.25 if yes, 0 if the harm is absent or only vaguely gestured at.

sub_criteria.name: Action and harm are adapted to the SmartCity scenario
sub_criteria.description: The action and its harmful consequence reference specific SmartCity infrastructure, services, or stakeholders — e.g., traffic sensors, waste-bin sensors, connected cameras, city dashboards, weather/heatwave alerts, 5G network, public app, city authorities, citizens using SmartCity services. A generic cyber-attack description (e.g., 'hackers steal data') that could apply to any system does NOT earn credit; the student must show how the attack exploits or disrupts SmartCity specifically.
sub_criteria.scoring_guidance: Award 0.25 per action (×3 = 0.75 total). For each action, check: does the description reference at least one concrete SmartCity component, service, or stakeholder group, AND does the described harm arise from the SmartCity context (not just a generic IT attack)? Award 0.25 if yes, 0 if the action/harm is generic or not tied to SmartCity.

findings.evidence: winner=B, confidence=0.92, a_score=0.67, b_score=0.75; Response B is stronger overall on the rubric. For action-category fit, B has three distinct actions that better match the motivation categories: ransom attack for Money, politically motivated traffic disruption for Politics, and anti-smart-tech belief-driven opposition for Ideas. In A, the Entertainment action repeats the dashboard-locking idea from Money, triggering the 'actions too similar' concern, and its harms are vaguer. For SmartCity-specific harmful impact, B gives clearer scenario-adapted consequences (service disruption, blocked emergency vehicles, trust damage), while A’s impacts are less specific and one action is explicitly uncertain. B’s Ideas example is weaker because it is more like online complaining than a concrete malicious action, but B still outperforms A across the criteria.
